item.title: 登录，我们都讨厌的事情 | Logging in, a thing we all hate
item.description:
我们都看过这个，对吧？

这是一个谎言。 而不仅仅是一个小家伙——一个大佬。 登录不在任何人的待办事项列表中。 它不会让任何人更接近他们的目标。 它不创造价值，也不提供服务。 登录，就像锁上你的前门一样，不是你想做的事情。 这是你不幸被迫做的事情，因为没有安全的选择。 它是用你没有要求的解决方案来解决你不感兴趣的问题。 并且它在黑桃中制造了其他问题。

登录表单

从 2018 年到 2021 年，我构建并维护了一个基于云的写作应用程序。 在那段时间里，有几千人登录。现在，登录不是重点。 这不应该是什么大不了的事。 它尽可能简单：一个电子邮件地址和一个密码（这里没有迷宫般的密码规则；12 个字符或更长，你很高兴）。 我手动构建了注册/登录/密码重置流程并对其进行了详尽的测试。

在这三年中，我收到了不到 10 封关于应用程序相对复杂的“写作”部分的客户支持电子邮件。 当然，偶尔会出现错误，但它们很少具有破坏性。 但是，我收到了数十封关于登录页面的支持电子邮件，其中包括两个文本字段和一个按钮！ 例如：

  • 有时人们会连续数次记错或输错密码。
  • 有时人们在注册时输入了错误的电子邮件地址，因此他们从未收到过帐户验证电子邮件。 它到了我不得不在“电子邮件”字段中禁止空格的地步，因为太多人不小心输入了空格。
  • 有时人们在注册时将他们的电子邮件或密码大写，然后在登录时输入小写。（密码在整个宇宙中的每个网站上都区分大小写，但我离题了。）

在上述所有情况下，他们都将 100% 的用户错误归咎于应用程序。 但是你能怪他们吗？ 世界上充斥着廉价、损坏的软件。 我自己也遇到过不少有问题的登录页面。

事情的丑陋事实是，登录——与软件产品交互的第一部分，整个体验都依赖于此——通常比应用程序所做的任何其他事情都更加复杂和令人担忧。 进入应用程序比做你想做的事情需要更多的努力。 这是一种侮辱仪式，是你脚上的一根刺，是你为了在线生存而必须应对的数百个其他人之上的轻微烦恼。

有没有更好的办法？

信任（是的，正确的）

您必须登录的原因是为了让其他人无法冒充您，对吗？ 想象一下，我们可以相信所有用户都以自己的身份登录。 没有密码，没有令牌。 告诉我们你是谁，你就准备好了。

有几个应用程序可以做到这一点。 例如，如果您以访客身份加入 Zoom 会议，它会询问您的姓名并显示您输入的任何内容。 或者，如果您曾经玩过 Fibbage 或 Quiplash（来自Jackbox Games的令人愉快的派对游戏），您也可以通过输入您的姓名来加入，而您的姓名未经任何验证。 对于您不拥有任何持久性数据并且错误识别您的风险相对较低的情况，这是合适的 UX。

但即使没有带星号的密码输入，人们也很挣扎。 他们拼错了自己的名字。 他们根本看不到“姓名”输入，并困惑地反复点击“开始”按钮，直到有人帮助他们。 当他们最终通过登录屏幕时，他们决定使用不同的名称，因此每个人都在等待他们退出并再次登录。 即使没有密码，登录也是错误的喜剧。

电子邮件甚至比名字更糟糕。 大多数人不了解电子邮件地址在语义上的工作原理。 这只是他们必须记住的一段文字，就像密码一样。 而且你不能假设普通人记得他们注册的地址——普通电子邮件用户有 1.75 个地址，我猜我这一代人的数字比我父母的要高得多。 我个人有数百个电子邮件别名。 如果不是我的密码管理器，我什至无法从我使用的大多数网站请求重置密码。

用户名和电子邮件一团糟。 不幸的是，它们只是混乱的一半。 对于大多数商业应用程序来说，免密码登录是不现实的。 任何时候用户在创建事物、为事物添加书签或对事物做出反应——即使是在化名下——他们都非常反对被冒充的想法。 更不用说当有人冒充您时造成的物质伤害，最糟糕的是当您的信用卡或政府身份证号受到威胁时。

单点登录

单点登录（SSO：“使用 Google 登录”、“使用 Facebook 登录”等）理论上比电子邮件密码登录更方便和安全。 您的身份由 SSO 提供商提供，他们向注册的第三方 API 提供令牌，因此您可以在登录一次后访问多个应用程序。 不过，根据我的经验，它并没有好很多。

也许我一个人在这里，但如果您的网站有多个 SSO 选项，我将不知道我用哪一个来创建我的帐户。 我会茫然地盯着登录屏幕片刻，随机选择一个选项，并希望最好。 如果 SSO 提供者请求允许共享我的身份，我会假设我选错了，关闭选项卡，然后尝试其他选项。 然后冲洗并重复，直到我认为这根本不值得麻烦。

我宁愿使用密码。

该链接中有一个令牌，可授予对应用程序的访问权限。 这是 SSO 和每次登录时重置密码之间的交叉点。这当然不会比其他任何人做的更糟糕 - 一般来说，您的电子邮件帐户是整个安全网络的单点故障 - 就用户体验而言太好了，因为 Slack 的大多数用户群都 24/7 全天候登录他们的电子邮件帐户。

但是，无论我们谈论的是 SSO 还是魔术链接，我们谈论的只是推卸责任。 这并不能解决问题。 它的意思是“希望其他人比我们做得更好。” 它的意思是“还记得 Facebook，安全和信任的典范吗？我们会让他们告诉我们你是谁。” 它把你所有的鸡蛋放在一个篮子里。

生物识别

面容 ID（主要）是一种使用乐趣。 我所要做的就是拿起我的手机。 它用红外摄像机扫描我的脸，如果它认为我是我，它就会让我进去。 默认情况下，它要求我实际查看它，因此在我睡着的时候没有人可以让自己进入。

我的最后一部手机有指纹识别器。 它糟透了。

据我观察，信息安全社区对生物识别技术持怀疑态度。 他们说“指纹是用户名，而不是密码”。 他们说“指纹是永远无法改变的半秘密”。 他们的推理是合理的：指纹，就像密码一样，只是通过电线发送的一系列位。 这些位可以由任何东西发送，真的。 电线的另一端不知道它们来自哪里。 因此，如果有人拥有您的指纹（或者，在 Face ID 的情况下，是您的面部照片）和正确的设备，那么欺骗生物识别登录几乎就像Mission Impossible所说的那样容易。 而且没有把精灵放回瓶子里——你不能“重置”你自己的皮肤。

也许生物特征认证的最大危险在于它看起来很安全。 我们长大后一遍又一遍地听到每个人的指纹都是独一无二的，没有两张脸完全相同。 而且由于我们通过面部识别彼此，我们直觉计算机可以这样做。 但“唯一”不等于“安全”。 当我们把不安全的东西当作安全的东西对待时，我们很容易忽视更重要的事情。 安全的假象只会使缺乏实际安全更加危险。

地点

我现在和以前的手机都依赖 PIN 码作为访问的备用方法。 这与“无密码登录”方法形成了有趣的对比。 手机本身就是用户名（只要我不恢复出厂设置）。 它或多或少永久地代表了一个单一的、静态的身份。 所以这是无用户名登录：我们只需要一个密码。 但即使它没有 PIN 或生物特征认证，即使任何人都可以拿起我的手机并使用它，它也会比我的大多数在线账户更安全，因为它需要个人、物理、现场访问. 我不必担心世界各地的骗子试图窃取我的东西； 我只需要担心任何距离我不到五英尺的人。 这就是 CTO 梦寐以求的那种安全。

当然，我的大部分数据并不是专门存储在手机的固态硬盘上。 还有很多东西让我夜不能寐。 但很高兴知道我的手机，不管它值多少钱，都是一个相对较小的风险。

谷歌和其他一些身份提供商通过使用位置作为附加的安全层来模拟这一点。 如果我正在旅行并尝试从新位置登录，我必须通过第二个因素进行身份验证，例如手机上的通知或受信任的联系人。 值得庆幸的是，这无法通过 VPN 或位置欺骗来规避，因为从新设备登录的处理方式相同。

位置和设备检查是一个很好的安全功能。 但它显然不能代替密码。 不仅因为其他人可以使用相同的设备或存在与我相同的位置，还因为我有时会去其他位置并使用其他设备。

密码管理器

我使用密码管理器并向所有人推荐。 确实没有更好的方法可以在整个互联网上使用强大的、唯一的密码而不会将自己锁在门外——尽管可以说，在某些情况下，经常被诽谤的“显示器上的便签”方法比“加密的”方法更安全密码库可从世界任何地方访问”方法。 无论如何，密码管理器是安全专家推荐的，我很乐意听从他们的专业知识。

密码管理器有两个严重的缺陷。

一方面，几乎没有人在 IT 之外使用它们，而且我们 IT 人员比我们的同行更注重安全。 所以密码管理器就像一家只接纳健康人的医院：他们帮助那些最不需要它的人，这使得他们在现实世界中的价值受到质疑。

其次，它们实际上并没有解决根本问题。 他们解决了由解决不同问题所产生的问题。 也就是说，如果安全是问题，那么密码就是解决方案，忘记密码是次要的。 密码管理器从这个问题中删除了两个步骤，部分是创可贴，部分是草率地为一个首先不足的解决方案提供理由。

因此，尽管它们对于任何关心在线安全的人来说可能是必不可少的工具，但它们并不是我们在这里讨论的问题的解决方案。

硬件认证设备

如果您正在寻找比密码管理器更不受欢迎的东西，那么 YubiKey 就是您的最佳选择。 在政府和大型科技公司之外，大多数人从未听说过硬件身份验证设备。 从理论上讲，它们是一个好主意，即使它们只不过是一个为您键入一次性密码的 USB 机器人键盘，但对于普通用户来说，它们很难卖。

另一方面，几乎每个人的钱包里都有一个：信用卡中支持 NFC 的微芯片。 芯片比信用卡号码和磁条更安全，因为它们可以即时执行计算，允许它们在销售点进行实时加密交换（真实故事！）。 信用卡号很容易被盗，但私钥几乎是防弹的，特别是当用户不知道它是什么并且他们的微芯片被设计为不会泄露它时。

如果您可以使用信用卡微芯片作为身份验证设备，那么您将进入安全天堂。 您的“密码”将是令人难忘的，几乎无法破解，并且只会受到物理盗窃或损坏。 即使您愿意，也不能通过电话将其交给诈骗者。 它将在设定的时间后过期并被替换。 而且由于每个人都知道如果他们的信用卡被盗会打电话给他们的银行，因此会内置缓解措施。几乎所有的安全风险都会转移到银行的信用卡取消和发行部门，他们必须非常努力才能做到这一点比普通消费者还差。

所以，是的，使用您的信用卡微芯片作为密码会很棒。 只有一个问题：你不能。 大多数计算机没有 NFC 芯片。 即使他们这样做了，我们也必须说服银行在某种程度上充当 SSO 提供商，他们可能有兴趣也可能不感兴趣。

密码

所以现在我们回到了我们开始的地方。 安全性、可移植性、便利性和易于实施的交汇点是密码。 这似乎是一种必要的邪恶。 但请不要误会：尽管有必要，但它非常非常邪恶。

密码的性质鼓励失败。 科技行业以外的大多数人更担心忘记密码而不是密码被盗。 当后者的后果如此严重时，这怎么可能呢？ 原因是双重的。 首先，被黑客入侵就像被抢劫或让你的房子被飓风摧毁：告诉自己这不会发生在你身上是非常容易的。 毕竟，到目前为止，它只发生在其他人身上。 其次，人们总是忘记密码。 去看看你妈妈或爸爸的 Facebook 提要，有很多关于它的模因。 这是一个永远不会感到遥远的威胁。 所以这是人们认真对待的一个问题，他们要么对所有事情都使用一个密码，从大通银行到企鹅俱乐部，要么保留一个由三四个同样弱密码组成的轮换列表，这真的好不了多少。

底线：每个密码都存在于从“弱而方便”到“强而烦人”的范围内。 我们不应该仅仅因为这样做是正确的，就期望用户行为趋向于“强烈而烦人”。 任何研究人类行为的人都会毫不犹豫地告诉你这个故事是如何结束的。

同样重要的是，密码不能证明任何事情。 充其量你可以希望它是由创建它的人输入的，你可以进一步希望那个人就是他们所说的那个人。 但它可能已被大规模零售黑客抢购或被短信诈骗者通过网络钓鱼。 它可能是从用户从未涉足的国家/地区暴力破解或键入的。 密码不是加密对话，它与身份无关。 它更像是我们隐藏起来的第二个用户名，因为我们真的不知道还能做什么。

那么，auth 的未来是什么？ 我非常希望这不是一个更长的密码和更多轮哈希的故事。 这种猫捉老鼠的游戏对任何人都不起作用。 如果我按照自己的方式进行，它将是生物识别技术（替换用户名字段）和带有非对称密钥的 NFC 微芯片的组合，最好是我现在信用卡上的那个（替换密码字段）。 如果 Apple 或 Twitter 或 Wells Fargo 的好人想要在那里进行一些基于位置或设备的验证，那就更好了。 我知道这项技术还没有准备好。 但它正在路上。



原文:

We've all seen this one, right?

"As a user, I want to log in to the app."

That's a lie. And not just a little one—a whopper. Logging in isn't on anyone's to-do list. It doesn't put anyone closer to their goals. It doesn't create value and it doesn't provide a service. Logging in, like locking your front door, isn't something you want to do. It's something you're haplessly forced to do because there's no safe alternative. It's meeting a problem you're not interested in with a solution you didn't ask for. And it's creating other problems in spades.

The login form

I built and maintained a cloud-based writing app from 2018-2021. Over that period of time a few thousand people logged in. Now, logging in wasn't the point. It wasn't supposed to be a big deal. It was as simple as it could possibly be: an email address and a password (no labyrinth of password rules here; 12 characters or longer and you were good to go). I built the signup/login/password reset flows by hand and tested them exhaustively.

During those three years, I received fewer than 10 customer support emails about the relatively complex "writing" part of the app. Occasionally there were bugs, of course, but they were rarely disruptive. However, I received dozens of support emails about the login page, which consisted of two text fields and a button! For example:

  • Sometimes people misremembered or mistyped their password several times in a row.
  • Sometimes people mistyped their email address when they were signing up, so they never got their account verification email. It got to the point where I had to ban spaces in the "email" field because so many people were typing them by accident.
  • Sometimes people capitalized their email or password when signing up, then typed them lowercase when logging in. (Passwords are case sensitive on every single website in the whole universe, but I digress.)

In all of the above cases, they blamed the app for something that was 100% user error. But can you blame them? The world is chock-full of cheap, broken software. I've encountered more than a few buggy login pages myself.

The ugly truth of the matter is that logging in—the very first part of interacting with a software product, something on which the entire experience depends—is often more complicated and fraught than anything else the app does. It takes more effort to get into the app than to do the thing you want to do with it. It's a hazing ritual, a thorn in your foot, a mild annoyance on top of the hundreds of others you have to cope with just to exist online.

Is there a better way?

Trust (yeah, right)

The reason you have to log in is so that no one else can pretend to be you, right? Imagine for a moment that we could trust all users to log in as themselves. No passwords, no tokens. Tell us who you are and you're all set.

A couple of apps do this. For example, if you join a Zoom meeting as a guest, it'll ask for your name and display whatever you type. Or if you've ever played Fibbage or Quiplash (delightful party games from Jackbox Games), you likewise join by entering your name, which isn't verified in any way. This is appropriate UX for contexts where you don't own any persistent data and the risk of misidentifying you is relatively low.

But even when there's no asterisk-masked password input, people struggle. They misspell their own names. They don't see the "Name" input at all and tap repeatedly on the "Start" button, bewildered, until someone helps them. When they finally pass the login screen they decide they'd rather go by a different name, so everyone waits for them to sign out and sign in again. Even without passwords, logging in is a comedy of errors.

Emails are even worse than names. Most people don't understand how an email address works, semantically; it's just a block of text they have to memorize, like a password. And you can't assume the average person remembers which address they signed up with—the average email user has 1.75 addresses, and I'd guess the number is much higher for my generation than my parents'. Personally I've got hundreds of email aliases. If it weren't for my password manager, I wouldn't even be able to request a password reset from most of the sites I use.

Usernames and emails are a mess. Unfortunately, they're only half the mess. A password-free login isn't realistic for most commercial apps. Any time users are creating things, bookmarking things, or reacting to things—even under a pseudonym—they're very averse to the idea of being impersonated. Not to mention the material harm that results when someone does impersonate you, worst of all when your credit card or government identification number are at stake.

SSO

Single Sign-On (SSO: "Sign in with Google," "Sign in with Facebook," etc.) is theoretically more convenient and secure than an email-password login. Your identity resides with the SSO provider and they provide tokens to registered third-party APIs so you can access multiple apps after logging in once. In my experience, though, it's not a whole lot better.

Maybe I'm alone here, but if your site has more than one SSO option I'm gonna have no idea which one I used to create my account. I'll stare blankly at the login screen for a few moments, pick an option at random, and hope for the best. If the SSO provider asks for permission to share my identity, I'm going to assume I picked wrong, close the tab, and try a different one. Then rinse and repeat until I decide it's not worth the trouble after all.

I'd rather use a password.

Slack has an interesting approach to this: when you sign in, you type your account email and they send you a "magic sign-in link." The link has a token in it that grants access to the app. It's a cross between SSO and resetting your password every time you log in. This certainly isn't worse than what anyone else does—your email account is a single point of failure for your entire security network, generally speaking—and in terms of UX it's excellent, since most of Slack's user base is signed into their email account 24/7.

But whether we're talking about SSO or magic links, we're talking about little more than passing the buck. This isn't solving the problem. It's saying "hopefully someone else is doing this better than we are." It's saying "remember Facebook, that paragon of security and trust? We're going to let them tell us who you are." It's putting all your eggs in one basket.

Biometrics

Face ID is (mostly) a joy to use. All I have to do is hold up my phone. It scans my face with an infrared camera and lets me in if it thinks I'm me. And by default it requires me to actually look at it, so no one can let themself in while I'm asleep.

My last phone had a fingerprint reader. It sucked.

The infosec community, from what I've observed, is skeptical of biometrics. They say "fingerprints are usernames, not passwords." They say "fingerprints are half-secrets that can't ever be changed." And their reasoning is sound: a fingerprint, like a password, is just a series of bits sent over a wire. Those bits can be sent by anything, really. The other end of the wire doesn't know where they came from. So if someone has your fingerprints (or, in the case of Face ID, a picture of your face) and the right equipment, fooling the biometric login is almost as easy as Mission Impossible makes it out to be. And there's no putting the genie back in the bottle—you can't "reset" your own skin.

Perhaps the greatest danger of biometric authentication is that it seems secure. We grow up hearing over and over again that everyone's fingerprints are unique and no two faces are exactly the same. And since we recognize each other by our faces, we intuit that it's okay for computers to do so. But "unique" is not the same as "secure." When we treat something insecure as if it were secure, we're prone to neglect more important things. The illusion of security only makes a lack of actual security more dangerous.

Location

Both my current and previous phone have relied on a PIN number as a backup method of access. This is an interesting contrast to the "passwordless login" method. The phone itself is the username (as long as I don't do a factory reset). It represents a single, static identity more or less permanently. So this is username-less login: all we need is a password. But even if it didn't have a PIN or biometric authentication, even if anyone at all could pick up my phone and use it, it would be more secure than most of my online accounts because it requires personal, physical, on-location access. I don't have to worry about scammers all across the world trying to steal my stuff; I only have to worry about anyone who comes within five feet of me. That's the kind of security CTO's dream about.

Of course, most of my data isn't stored exclusively on my phone's solid state drive. There's still plenty to keep me up at night. But it's nice to know that my phone, for whatever it's worth, is a relatively small risk.

Google and a few other identity providers simulate this by using location as an added layer of security. If I'm traveling and try to sign in from a new location, I have to authenticate via a second factor like a notification on my phone or a trusted contact. And thankfully, this can't be circumvented by VPN or location spoofing because signing in from a new device is treated the same way.

Location and device checking is a nice security feature. But it obviously can't replace passwords. Not just because other people could use the same device or exist in the same location as me, but because I sometimes go to other locations and use other devices.

Password managers

I use a password manager and recommend it to everyone. There's really not a better way to use strong, unique passwords across the entire internet without ever locking yourself out—though it could be argued that the oft-maligned "sticky note on your monitor" method is more secure in some contexts than the "encrypted password vault accessible from anywhere in the world" method. Regardless, password managers are what security experts recommend and I'm happy to defer to their expertise.

Password managers have two serious flaws.

For one, practically no one uses them outside of IT, and we IT folks are more security-conscious than our peers anyway. So password managers are like a hospital that only admits the healthy: they help those who need it least, which makes their value in the real world questionable.

Second, they don't actually solve an essential problem. They solve a problem created by the solution to a different problem. That is, if security is the problem, then passwords are the solution and forgetting your password is secondary to it. Password managers are two steps removed from the issue, part band-aid and part hasty justification for a solution that's insufficient in the first place.

So while they may be an essential tool for anyone who cares about online security, they're not a solution to the problem we're discussing here.

Hardware authentication devices

If you're looking for something even more unpopular than password managers, look no further than YubiKey. Outside of government and Big Tech most people have never heard of hardware auth devices. They're a good idea in theory, even when they amount to little more than a USB robot-keyboard that types a one-time password for you, but they're a hard sell for the average user.

On the other hand, pretty much everyone has one in their wallet: the NFC-enabled microchip in your credit card. Chips are more secure than credit card numbers and magnetic stripes because they can perform computations on the fly, allowing them to do live cryptographic exchanges at the point of sale (true story!). Credit card numbers are easily stolen but a private key is just about bulletproof, especially when the user doesn't know what it is and their microchip is engineered not to divulge it.

If you could use a credit card microchip as an authentication device, you'd be in security paradise. Your "password" would be unforgettable, nearly unhackable, and vulnerable only to physical theft or damage. You couldn't give it away to a scammer over the phone even if you wanted to. It would expire and be replaced after a set amount of time. And since everyone knows to call their bank if their credit card gets stolen, mitigation would come built in. Almost all the security risks would be shifted to the bank's credit card cancellation and issuance department, and they'd have to try pretty hard to be worse at it than the average consumer.

So yeah, it would be awesome to use your credit card microchip as a password. There's only one problem: you can't. Most computers don't have NFC chips. And even if they did, we'd have to convince banks to act as SSO providers to some extent, which they may or may not be interested in doing.

Passwords

So now we're back where we started. At the intersection of security, portability, convenience, and ease of implementation is the password. It seems it's a necessary evil. But make no mistake: for all its necessity, it's very, very evil.

The nature of the password encourages failure. Most people outside the tech industry are far more worried about forgetting their password than having it stolen. How could that be, when the consequences of the latter are so much more severe? The reason is twofold. First, getting hacked is like getting mugged or having your house torn apart by a hurricane: it's extremely easy to tell yourself it won't happen to you. After all, it's only happened to other people so far. And second, people forget passwords all the time. Go check your mom or dad's Facebook feed, there's an abundance of memes about it. AJR even writes lyrics on the subject. It's a threat that never feels distant. So that's the one people take seriously, and they respond by either using one password for everything, from Chase Bank to Club Penguin, or keeping a rotating list of three or four equally weak passwords, which really isn't much better.

The bottom line: every password exists on a spectrum from "weak and convenient" to "strong and annoying." And we should not expect user behavior to trend toward "strong and annoying" just because it's the right thing to do. Anyone who studies human behavior at any scale will tell you, without hesitation, how that story ends.

Of equal importance is the fact that a password doesn't prove anything. At best you can hope it was typed by the person who created it, and you can further hope that person is who they said they were. But it might have been snapped up in a massive retail hack or phished by an SMS scammer. It might have been brute-forced or typed from a country where the user has never set foot. A password is not a cryptographic conversation and it has no connection to an identity. It's more like a second username that we act secretive about because we don't really know what else to do.

What's the future of auth, then? I desperately hope it isn't a tale of ever-longer passwords and more rounds of hashing. That cat-and-mouse game isn't working for anyone. If I had my way, it would be a combination of biometrics (replacing the username field) and an NFC microchip with an asymmetric key, preferably the one on my credit card right now (replacing the password field). And if the good folks at Apple or Twitter or Wells Fargo want to throw some location- or device-based verification on there, so much the better. I know the technology isn't ready yet. But it's on its way.
item.author: isaacdlyman

item.title: MindsDB 教程：预测书籍的类型 | MindsDB Tutorial: Predicting the Genre of Books
item.description:
本教程的目标是根据书的概要预测书的类型。 唯一的先决知识是 SQL 和数据库的基础知识。 机器学习的知识是一个奖励，但不是一个交易破坏者。

工作流程分为 3 个主要步骤：

  1. 连接到数据库
  2. 触发 SQL 查询以创建和交易 ML 预测器。
  3. 训练完成后，执行另一个 SQL 查询以使用训练过的预测器进行预测。

开发环境

由于 MindsDB 提供云版本，我们可以在这里注册，也可以通过Pip或Docker在本地使用它。 在本教程中，我们将通过云使用 MindsDB 来强调访问的便利性。
唯一的缺点是数据中的行数有上限。 数据文件的最大大小限制为 10MB。

数据设置

数据

我们正在使用来自 Kaggle 的 Book Genre Prediction 数据集，可在此处下载。

关于数据

此数据集包含一个名为 data.csv 的文件，其中包含数据。 数据分为 4 列，索引、标题、流派、摘要。 数据大约10MB，有4656条记录。 数据格式如下。

标题类型概括
星期三淹死幻想周三溺水者是 Morrow Days 中第一位站在 Arthur 一边并希望...
紫罗兰是蓝色的惊悚片这本书开始于玫瑰是红色的，以亚历克斯·克罗斯博士在被谋杀的联邦调查局特工贝...
星际穿越科学主角亚瑟（“艺术”）木比和他的姐姐默特尔被邀请到 ...

连接到数据源

在本教程中，我们以 MindsDB 云上的 CSV 文件形式连接到我们的数据库。 我们首先在此处下载 csv 数据文件并上传文件“data.csv”。 按照指南将文件上传到 MindsDB 并适当地命名表。 我用过“书流派”。

上传后，您可以直接在数据库上运行查询。 让我们从预览我们将用于训练模型的数据开始：
SELECT * FROM files.BookGenres LIMIT 10;

对于显示数据分布的数据可视化，请单击DATA INSIGHTS 。

训练

让我们创建和训练机器学习模型。 为此，我们使用CREATE PREDICTOR语法，在其中我们指定要训练的查询以及要学习的 PREDICT 内容。 在这种情况下，我们将使用表中的“title”、“genre”和“summary”特征/列来预测“genre”。

CREATE PREDICTOR mindsdb.predict_genre_model FROM files (SELECT title, genre, summary FROM BookGenres) PREDICT genre;

培训可能需要几分钟才能完成。 您可以像这样监控模型的状态：

SELECT * FROM mindsdb.predictors WHERE name='predict_genre_model';

该查询列出了用于训练数据的候选模型以及性能和准确性等其他指标。

要查看 MindsDB 的 AutoML 管道使用的参数，请执行以下 SQL：

DESCRIBE PREDICTOR mindsdb.predict_genre_model.ensemble;
结果是一个 JSON，列出了用于训练候选预测器的超参数和值。

做出预测

训练完成后，您的模型就可以进行预测了。 使用 MindsDB 进行预测就像运行 SQL 查询一样简单。

SELECT genre FROM mindsdb.predict_genre_model WHERE summary = "The Prince of no value Brishen Khaskem, prince of the Kai, has lived content as the nonessential spare heir to a throne secured many times over. A trade and political alliance between the human kingdom of Gaur and the Kai kingdom of Bast-Haradis requires that he marry a Gauri woman to seal the treaty. Always a dutiful son, Brishen agrees to the marriage and discovers his bride is as ugly as he expected and more beautiful than he could have imagined. The noblewoman of no importance Ildiko, niece of the Gauri king, has always known her only worth to the royal family lay in a strategic marriage. Resigned to her fate, she is horrified to learn that her intended groom isn't just a foreign aristocrat but the younger prince of a people neither familiar nor human. Bound to her new husband, Ildiko will leave behind all she's known to embrace a man shrouded in darkness but with a soul forged by light. Two people brought together by the trappings of duty and politics will discover they are destined for each other, even as the powers of a hostile kingdom scheme to tear them apart. (less)";

结论

即使不是数据科学家或没有任何机器学习的正式知识，也很容易开始使用机器学习进行预测。 我花了不到一个小时的时间让我的模型启动并运行。 随意检查一下。 文档写得很好，开发者社区也在不断改进产品。



原文:

MindsDB introduces AI and machine learning into databases to help data teams – and every day data users – identify patterns, predict trends, and train models.

The goal of this tutorial is to predict the genre of the book based on the synopsis of the book. The only pre-requisite knowledge is the basics of SQL and databases. Knowledge of Machine learning is a bonus but not a deal-breaker.

The workflow is divided into 3 main steps:

  1. Connect to the database
  2. Fire an SQL query to create and trade an ML predictor.
  3. Once training is complete, execute another SQL query to make predictions using the trained predictor.

Development Environment

As MindsDB offers a cloud version, we can sign up here, or we could use it locally via Pip or Docker. In this tutorial, we will be using MindsDB via the cloud to highlight the ease of access.
The only drawback is the upper limit on the number of rows in the data. MindsDB supports up to 10K rows and will raise an error for a larger number. Data files are restricted to a maximum size of 10MB.

Data Setup

Data

We are using the Book Genre Prediction data set from Kaggle, downloadable here .

About the data

This dataset contains a single file named data.csv containing the data. Data is divided across 4 columns, index , title, genre, summary. The data is about 10MB and has 4656 records. Data is of the following format.

# index title genre summary
1 Drowned Wednesday fantasy Drowned Wednesday is the first Trustee among the Morrow Days who is on Arthur's side and wishes the...
2 Violets are Blue thriller The book begins where Roses are Red ended, with Dr. Alex Cross at the home of murdered FBI Agent Be...
3 Starcross science Protagonist Arthur ("Art") Mumby and his older sister Myrtle are invited to the Starcross hotel on ...

Connecting to the datasource

For this tutorial, we connect to our database as a CSV file on the MindsDB cloud. We begin by downloading the csv data file here and uploading the file "data.csv".  Follow the guide to upload the file to MindsDB and name the table appropriately. I have used "BookGenres".

Once uploaded, you can run queries directly on the database. Let's start by previewing the data we will use to train the model:
SELECT * FROM files.BookGenres LIMIT 10;

For a data visualization, showing the distribution of data, click on DATA INSIGHTS.

Training

Let's create and train the machine learning model. For that, we use the CREATE PREDICTOR syntax, where we specify what query to train FROM and what we want to learn to PREDICT. In this case, we will train using the 'title' ,'genre' and 'summary' features/columns in the table to predict the 'genre'.

CREATE PREDICTOR mindsdb.predict_genre_model FROM files (SELECT title, genre, summary FROM BookGenres) PREDICT genre;

It may take a couple of minutes for the training to complete. You can monitor the status of your model like this:

SELECT * FROM mindsdb.predictors WHERE name='predict_genre_model';

The AutoML will figure out the model, and you can use DESCRIBE to see how this model was built. MindsDB uses several candidate models to internally train the data and then picks up the most optimised one as the result model to make the predictions.

DESCRIBE PREDICTOR mindsdb.predict_genre_model.model;
The query lists out the candidate models used to train the data along with other metrics like performance, and accuracy.

To see the parameters used by MindsDB's AutoML pipeline execute the following SQL:

DESCRIBE PREDICTOR mindsdb.predict_genre_model.ensemble;
The result is a JSON listing the hyper-parameters and values used for training your candidate predictor.

Making Predictions

Once training is completed, your model is ready to predict. Prediction using MindsDB is easy as running an SQL query.

SELECT genre FROM mindsdb.predict_genre_model WHERE summary = "The Prince of no value Brishen Khaskem, prince of the Kai, has lived content as the nonessential spare heir to a throne secured many times over. A trade and political alliance between the human kingdom of Gaur and the Kai kingdom of Bast-Haradis requires that he marry a Gauri woman to seal the treaty. Always a dutiful son, Brishen agrees to the marriage and discovers his bride is as ugly as he expected and more beautiful than he could have imagined. The noblewoman of no importance Ildiko, niece of the Gauri king, has always known her only worth to the royal family lay in a strategic marriage. Resigned to her fate, she is horrified to learn that her intended groom isn’t just a foreign aristocrat but the younger prince of a people neither familiar nor human. Bound to her new husband, Ildiko will leave behind all she’s known to embrace a man shrouded in darkness but with a soul forged by light. Two people brought together by the trappings of duty and politics will discover they are destined for each other, even as the powers of a hostile kingdom scheme to tear them apart. (less)";

Conclusions

It is very easy to start making predictions with machine learning even without being a data scientist or having any formal knowledge of machine learning. It took me under an hour to get my model up and running. Tools like MindsDB take away the burden of choosing the right model, the right parameters and training time so you can focus on the problem at hand and not the technical aspects.

Feel free to check this for yourself. The documentation is well written and the developer community is constantly improving the product.
item.author: f2010126

item.title: 在 Spring 中使用大型 JSON 响应 | Consume Large JSON Response in Spring
item.description:
在上一篇文章中，在 Spring 中流式处理大型 JSON 响应。 我们生成了大 json 作为响应。 在本文中，我们将使用该响应，而不会出现堆内存溢出。

目标

在这篇文章中，唯一的目标是消耗那个大的 json 响应，并作为练习做一些基本的计算，作为关于接收 json 响应的概念证明。

作为参考，我们从服务器应用程序收到以下 json 内容

{ "employees" : [ { "empNo" : 10001 , "birthDate" : "02 September 1953" , "firstName" : "Georgi" , "lastName" : "Facello" , "gender" : "M" , "hireDate" : "26 June 1986" } ... ~ 300 K employee entries ]
}

我们将计算以下

  • 女性员工人数。
  • 男性员工人数。
  • 员工总数。

注意：对员工名单进行的统计仅用于演示目的，与现实世界的情况并不接近。 不要从字面上看这些例子，比如从员工列表中计算男性和女性员工的总数并不是理想的方法。 使用聚合函数在数据库端更好地计算。

我们将使用创建一个简单的“Spring Web 应用程序”

  • 春季启动（2.7.4）
  • 春季网。
  • 百里香叶。
  • 执行

    从 enum 和 pojo 声明开始

    性别.java

     public enum Gender { M , F ; }

    雇员.java

     @Getter @Setter @NoArgsConstructor public class Employee { private Long empNo ; private Date birthDate ; private String firstName ; private String lastName ; private Gender gender ; private Date hireDate ; }

    这里没什么特别的，只是一个 stats pojo，它将通过一些方便的计数方法来保存所有的统计数据。

    我们将使用RestTemplate进行 HTTP 通信。 所以让我们为RestTemplate配置一个 bean

    这里需要注意的重要一点是，我们为转换器MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter自定义了日期格式，以与 json 响应日期格式保持一致。

    一个额外的ObjectMapper bean 也从RestTemplate的转换器中暴露出来。 接下来我们将需要它，同时反序列化 json 响应。

    逐块接收响应

    现在是整个写作中最赚钱的部分，做 HTTP 请求并接收响应。 从之前的文章Streaming Large JSON Response in Spring中可以明显看出，在 pojo 中加载整个响应会耗尽堆内存。 在这里，我们不会一次收到整个响应，我们会将JsonParser连接到响应的输入流中，并根据需要解析接收到的 json。 因此，我们将与整个响应一起移动，而不会将其完全加载到内存中。

    实际上这里发生了什么，

    • 我们使用RestTemplate发起 HTTP 请求并传递一个ResponseExtractor实现。
    • 使用之前配置的objectMapper和响应正文中的inputStream准备了一个JsonParser 。
    • 然后进行一些琐碎的检查（对象开始{令牌检查，开始字段"employees"令牌，数组开始[令牌），然后我们开始一个接一个地读取employee对象，直到我们找到数组令牌的结尾] 。
    • 我们将解析后的employee对象传递给消费者，以根据被调用者的需要处理员工，并进行一些简单的计数以便稍后返回。
    • 最后close() jsonParser 。

    使用响应

    现在是时候根据收到的员工一一计算统计数据了

    上面的方法几乎是自我解释的。 它使用我们之前声明的fetchAllEmployee方法来获取所有员工并根据声明的规则进行计数。 为了跟踪准备统计数据所需的时间，我们还计算了生成统计数据所需的秒数。

    现在用于将统计信息呈现到浏览器中的控制器

    和模板

    员工-stats.html

     ... Html <table border= "6" > <thead> <th> Reference </th> <th> Value </th> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <!-- Because ladies first :) --> <td> Female Employee Count </td> <td class= "value" th:text= "${stat.femaleEmployeeCount}" ></td> </tr> <tr> <td> Male Employee Count </td> <td class= "value" th:text= "${stat.maleEmployeeCount}" ></td> </tr> <tr> <td> Total Employee Count </td> <td class= "value" th:text= "${stat.totalEmployeeCount}" ></td> </tr> <tr> <td> Preparation Delay </td> <td class= "value" th:text= "${stat.reportGenerationDelayInSeconds + ' seconds'}" ></td> </tr> </tbody> </table> ... More Html

    最后一件事是

    所以我们的消费者应用程序在端口8081上运行。

    输出

    服务器和消费者应用程序都使用 jvm arg -Xmx32m运行以演示堆大小限制。

    显然，由于我们计算这些值的方式，响应花费的时间比预期的要长。 此示例仅用于演示目的并保持本文的范围较小。

    以上所有代码都可以在我的github 存储库中找到。

    将来将介绍此方法的适当用例（从提取的 json 下载原始csv报告）。



原文:

In the previous post Streaming Large JSON Response in Spring. we generated large json as response. In this article, we will consuming that response, without heap memory overflow.

Goal

In this post, the sole target is to consume that large json response and as an exercise do some basic calculation as a proof of concept about receiving the json response.

As a reference we receive following json contents from the server application

{ "employees" : [ { "empNo":10001, "birthDate":"02 September 1953", "firstName":"Georgi", "lastName":"Facello", "gender":"M", "hireDate":"26 June 1986" } ... ~300K employee entries ]
}

We will be counting following

  • Number of female employee.
  • Number of male employee.
  • Total number of employee.

NB: The statistics done with the employee list is for demonstration purpose and no way near to real world scenario. Do not take these examples here literally, like counting the number of total, male and female employees from employee list is not the ideal way. Better calculate at database end using aggregate functions.

We will be creating a simple "Spring Web Application" using

  • Spring Boot (2.7.4)
  • Spring Web.
  • Thymeleaf.
  • Lombok and Spring Boot Devtools.

Implementation

Start with the enum and pojo declaration

Gender.java

public enum Gender { M,F;
}

Employee.java

@Getter
@Setter
@NoArgsConstructor
public class Employee { private Long empNo; private Date birthDate; private String firstName; private String lastName; private Gender gender; private Date hireDate;
}

EmployeeStats.java

@Getter
@Setter
@NoArgsConstructor
public class EmployeeStats { private Long totalEmployeeCount; private Long femaleEmployeeCount = 0L; private Long maleEmployeeCount = 0L; private Long reportGenerationDelayInSeconds; public void incrementFemaleEmployeeCount() { femaleEmployeeCount++; } public void incrementMaleEmployeeCount() { maleEmployeeCount++; }
}

Nothing fancy here, just a stats pojo, which will be holding all the statistics with some convenient method for doing the counting.

We will be using RestTemplate for the HTTP communication. So lets configure a bean for RestTemplate

WebConfig.java

@Configuration
public class WebConfig { @Bean public RestTemplate restTemplate() { RestTemplate restTemplate = new RestTemplate(); for(HttpMessageConverter<?> converter : restTemplate.getMessageConverters()) { if(converter instanceof MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter) { MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter jacksonConverter = (MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter) converter; ObjectMapper objectMapper = jacksonConverter.getObjectMapper(); objectMapper.setDateFormat(new SimpleDateFormat("dd MMMM YYYY")); } } return restTemplate; } @Bean public ObjectMapper restTemplateObjectMapper(RestTemplate restTemplate) { for(HttpMessageConverter<?> converter : restTemplate.getMessageConverters()) { if(converter instanceof MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter) { return ((MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter) converter).getObjectMapper(); } } return null; }
}

Important thing to notice here, we customised the date format for converter MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter to align with the json response date format.

An extra bean of ObjectMapper is also exposed from the RestTemplate's converter. We will be needing that next, while deserialising the json response.

Receiving response chunk by chunk

Now the most lucrative part of this whole writing, doing the HTTP request and receive the response. From the previous article Streaming Large JSON Response in Spring it is obvious that, loading the whole response in a pojo will exhaust the heap memory. Here we will not receive the whole response at once, we will connect a JsonParser into the response's input stream and parse received json as per our need. Thus we will be moving along with the whole response without actually loading it fully in the memory.

EmployeeReportService.java

@Service
@RequiredArgConstruct
public class EmployeeReportService { private final RestTemplate restTemplate; private final ObjectMapper objectMapper; public Long fetchAllEmployee(Consumer<Employee> employeeConsumer) { String url = "http://localhost:8080/employees?stream=true"; return restTemplate.execute(url, HttpMethod.GET, null, (response) -> { Long employeeCount = 0L; JsonParser jsonParser = objectMapper.getFactory().createParser(response.getBody()); if(jsonParser.nextToken() == JsonToken.START_OBJECT) { if(jsonParser.nextFieldName() == "employees") { if(jsonParser.nextToken() == JsonToken.START_ARRAY) { while(jsonParser.nextToken() != JsonToken.END_ARRAY) { Employee employee = jsonParser.readValueAs(Employee.class); employeeConsumer.accept(employee); employeeCount++; } } } } jsonParser.close(); return employeeCount; }); }
}

Actually what is happening here,

  • We initiate the HTTP request using the RestTemplate and pass a ResponseExtractor implementation.
  • The ResponseExtractor implementation starts with getting the inputStream from the response body.
  • Prepared a JsonParser using the objectMapper configured before and inputStream from the response body.
  • Then some trivial checking (starting of object { token check, starting the field "employees" token, starting of the array [ token), then we start reading the employee object one by one, until we find the end of the array token ].
  • We pass the parsed employee object to the consumer, to process the employee as per the callee need and doing some trivial counting to return later.
  • At the end close() the jsonParser.

Using the response

Now time to calculate the statistics based on the received employee one by one

EmployeeReportService.java

@Service
public class EmployeeReportService { .... Previous Code public EmployeeStats prepareEmployeeStats() { final Long startTime = System.currentTimeMillis(); final EmployeeStats employeeStats = new EmployeeStats(); final Long totalEmployeeCount = fetchAllEmployee((employee) -> { switch (employee.getGender()) { case F: employeeStats.incrementFemaleEmployeeCount(); break; case M: employeeStats.incrementMaleEmployeeCount(); break; } }); final Long endTime = System.currentTimeMillis(); employeeStats.setReportGenerationDelayInSeconds((endTime - startTime) / 1000); employeeStats.setTotalEmployeeCount(totalEmployeeCount); return employeeStats; }
}

Above method is pretty much self explained. It's using our previously declared fetchAllEmployee method to fetch all the employee and counting based on declared rule. To keep track of the required time to prepare the statistics, we also calculated the number of seconds required to generate the statistics.

Now the controller for rendering the statistics into the browser

EmployeeController.java

@Controller
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class EmployeeController {

    private final EmployeeReportService employeeReportService;

    @GetMapping("/employee-stats")
    public String getEmployeeStats(Model model) {
        EmployeeStats employeeStats = employeeReportService.prepareEmployeeStats();
        model.addAttribute("stat", employeeStats);
        return "employee-stats";
    }
}

and the template

employee-stats.html


... Html <table border="6"> <thead> <th>Reference</th> <th>Value</th> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <!-- Because ladies first :) --> <td>Female Employee Count</td> <td class="value" th:text="${stat.femaleEmployeeCount}"></td> </tr> <tr> <td>Male Employee Count</td> <td class="value" th:text="${stat.maleEmployeeCount}"></td> </tr> <tr> <td>Total Employee Count</td> <td class="value" th:text="${stat.totalEmployeeCount}"></td> </tr> <tr> <td>Preparation Delay</td> <td class="value" th:text="${stat.reportGenerationDelayInSeconds + ' seconds'}"></td> </tr> </tbody>
</table>

... More Html

And the last thing is

So our consumer application is running on port 8081.

Output

Both Server & Consumer application is running with jvm arg -Xmx32m to demonstrate the heap size limitation.

Clearly the response took longer than the expected because of the way we are counting these values. This example is just for demonstration purpose and to keep the scope of this writing small.

All the code above can be found into my github repository.

In future an appropriate use case (downloading a raw csv report from the extracted json) of this approach will be covered.
item.author: ratulsharker

item.title: DevOps - 入门 | DevOps - Getting Started
item.description:
苏米特·贾迪亚

发表于它旨在缩短系统开发生命周期并提供具有高质量软件的持续交付。

为什么选择 DevOps？ 目前在组织中广泛使用的最流行的方法是敏捷。
但是敏捷方法主要关注产品（软件）开发，而不是发布/部署（服务器/基础设施）。

部署变得高效（至少快 50%）
  • 更少的失败
  • 更好的恢复时间

    • 文化在 DevOps 中最重要。 这将带来一整套实践，例如：

    • 筒仓的存在，
    • 团队协作。

    在 DevOps 中，人的价值高于过程而不是工具。

    注意：不要试图复制其他组织的做法，因为它适用于他们的组织。

    • 当我们谈论自动化时，我们会考虑以下概念：

  • 持续交付管道（例如，这里我们谈论的是 Jenkins）。

    • 持续交付管道实际上分为两部分：持续集成和持续交付。

    • 持续改进过程 (Kaizen) 是 DevOps 的核心。 但为此，仍然需要能够知道一个人是否有所改进并能够证明这一点。 没有奇迹，措施是关键，因为必须根据事实、数据而不是意见做出决定。

    • 共享包含三个组件：

    • 能见度

    • 透明度

    • 知识传输

    可见性使每个人都可以看到组织其他部分的进展。 具体来说，可见性可以让您知道一个团队的工作是否会给另一个团队带来问题。 它还支持早期反馈，因此尽早发现错误。

    透明度使每个人都能朝着共同的目标努力。

    知识转移旨在规避组织内的约束（可能通过团队之间共享知识）并促进集体智慧（我们集体比个人更聪明的事实）。

    工作总是流向一个方向——下游

    这一旅程的成果包括了解和服务所有内部和外部客户，缩短和加强所有反馈循环，以及在需要的地方嵌入知识。

    这条道路旨在建立一种鼓励两件事的文化：持续实验、冒险和从错误中学习。 了解重复和练习是掌握的先决条件。
    这是一种冒险的方式，因为失败的机会很高

    配置：服务器已准备好操作系统、软件和网络。

  • 部署：在服务器上添加或升级软件。

  • 编排：多个系统上的协调操作。 例如 Kubernetes 是一个编排工具

  • 配置管理：通过内存、空间、依赖软件等文件管理服务器配置。
    例如地形

  • 命令式（程序）：命令（逐步方法）产生所需的状态。

  • 声明式（程序式）：定义了所需的状态，工具将实现它。

  • 幂等：重复执行，结果相同。

  • 持续集成：每次签入时构建和单元测试。

  • 持续交付：在每次签入时部署在生产实时环境中。

  • 持续部署：在单元测试之后，将更改小批量部署到生产中。

    • 一旦暂停，sumitjadiya 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

    一旦解除暂停，sumitjadiya 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

    一旦未发布，sumitjadiya 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

    如果 sumitjadiya 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

    感谢您保护 DEV 社区👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 sumitjadiya 的方法：

    举报其他不当行为

    取消标记 sumitjadiya 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



    原文:

    Sumit Jadiya

    Posted on Oct 8

    The simple definition for DevOps is -

    DevOps is a set of practices that combine software development and IT operations. It aims to shorten the system development lifecycle and provide continuous delivery with high software quality.

    Why DevOps? (Need of DevOps)

    The current most popular methodology widely used in organisations is Agile.
    But the agile methodology mainly focusses on the product (software) development and not on the release/deployments (servers/infra).

    Benefits of DevOps Methodology

    • deployment becomes efficient (at least 50% faster)
    • less failure
    • better recovery time

    DevOps CAMS

    Culture is most important in DevOps. This will bring a whole set of practices such as :

    • the use of Scrum,
    • the presence of silos,
    • teams collaborations.

    In DevOps, People are valued over process over tools.

    Note: Don't try to copy the practices of other organisations just because it works in their organisations.

    • A for Automation Implement automation in such a way that it can be replicated.

      When we talk about automation, we think about the notions of:

  • “infrastructure as code” (for this we can use tools like Ansible or Chef)

  • continuous delivery pipelines (here we are talking about Jenkins for example).

    • Continuous delivery pipelines are in fact in two parts: Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery.

    • M for Measurement
      The continuous improvement process (Kaizen) is at the heart of DevOps. But for that, it is still necessary to be able to know if one has improved and to be able to prove it. No miracle, measures are the key, because decisions must be made on facts, data, not opinions.

    • S for Sharing
      Sharing has three components:

    • the visibility

    • the transparency

    • knowledge transfer

    Visibility allows everyone to see progress in other parts of the organisation. Specifically, visibility allows you to know if one team's work might cause problems for another team. It also enables early feedback, so bugs are caught sooner rather than later.

    Transparency allows everyone to work towards a common goal.

    Knowledge transfer aims to evade constraints (maybe by sharing knowledge between team) within an organisation and promote collective intelligence (the fact that we are more intelligent collectively than individually).

    DevOps - Three Ways

    Work always flows in one direction – downstream

    Outcomes of this journey include understanding and serving all internal and external customers, shortening and strengthening all feedback loops, and embedding knowledge where it is needed.

    This path aims to build a culture that encourages two things: Continuous experimentation, taking risks and learning from mistakes. Understanding that repetition and practice are prerequisites for mastery.
    This is Risky way as failure chances are high

    DevOps Lifecycle -

    DevOps Terms -

    • Provisioning : Server is ready with OS, software and networking.

    • Deployment : Adding or upgrading software on server.

    • Orchestrations : Coordinated operations on multiple systems. e.g. Kubernetes is an Orchestration tool

    • Configuration Management : Managing server configuration via files such as Ram, space, depending software etc.
      e.g. terraform

    • Imperative (Procedural) : command(step by step approach) to produce a desired state.

    • Declarative (Procedural) : desired state is defined and tools will achieve it.

    • Idempotent : repeat execution and same result.

    • Continuous Integration : build and unit test at every check-in.

    • Continuous Delivery : deploy on production live environment at every check-in.

    • Continuous Deployment : after unit testing, deploy changes to production in small batches.

    Once suspended, sumitjadiya will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

    Once unsuspended, sumitjadiya will be able to comment and publish posts again.

    Once unpublished, all posts by sumitjadiya will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

    If sumitjadiya is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

    Thanks for keeping DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻 safe. Here is what you can do to flag sumitjadiya:

    sumitjadiya consistently posts content that violates DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

    Report other inappropriate conduct

    Unflagging sumitjadiya will restore default visibility to their posts.
item.author: sumitjadiya

item.title: ASP.NET Core 身份：注册、登录和注销 | ASP.NET Core Identity: Registration, Login and Logout
item.description:
在这篇博文中，我们将研究如何在 Web 应用程序中实现 ASP.NET Core Identity 以添加身份验证和授权功能。 用户可以创建一个帐户并使用用户名和密码登录。 本文特别关注新手和任何想要学习或考虑在其 .NET 程序中使用 Identity 的新人。 这是一系列博客文章，因此请查看其他文章以获取更多信息。

什么是 ASP.NET Core 标识？

当我们构建应用程序时，关键因素之一是“如何处理用户管理？”，这意味着管理用户、登录、注销、密码、配置文件数据、角色、声明、令牌、电子邮件确认等。 因此，为了帮助简化这些用户管理过程，我们可以使用 ASP.NET Core Identity 作为会员系统。

开发设置

对于此示例，我们将使用 Visual Studio 2022 和 .NET 6.0 创建一个 ASP.NET Core MVC Web 应用程序

步骤 01：创建 ASP.NET Core Web 应用程序 (MVC) 时，我们不会选择身份验证类型并将其设置为“无”，因为我们将从头开始研究如何在应用程序中设置身份功能。 为简单起见，我们将取消选中“配置 HTTPS”和“启用 Docker”

创建后，我们将拥有一个没有任何身份验证功能的基于 MVC 的 Web 应用程序。

运行该项目将显示以下网页：

步骤02：现在我们将通过脚手架添加身份功能。 因此，右键单击项目并转到“添加”并选择“新建脚手架项目”。 在以下窗口中选择“身份”并按“添加”。 为了在脚手架上运行 nuget 包“Microsoft.VisualStudio.Web.CodeGeneration.Design”需要安装。 这将在添加脚手架项目时自动安装。

从以下选择我们要在我们的网络应用程序中添加的身份功能。 在本例中，我们将选择“登录”、“注销”、“注册”。 我们还需要指定以下内容：

  • 布局页面：身份文件的布局页面，因此，我们将选择我们现有的共享“_Layout.cshtml”页面，该页面默认由项目创建。
  • 数据上下文类：通过选择 + 按钮，它将生成上下文类名称，我们将其重命名为“AspNetCoreIdentityDbContext”
  • 用户类：通过选择 + 按钮，我们将用户模型类名称设置为“AspNetCoreIdentityUser”

点击“添加”现在我们的身份搭建好了。 而我们在项目中有如下结构变化

这里我们的数据库有“AspNetCoreIdentityDbContext”。 我们将通过它访问我们的 SQL Server。 然后我们有“AspNetCoreIdentityUser”，它继承自“IdentityUser”，它实现了登录、注销、注册等代码。 现在运行应用程序，我们需要添加“注册”“登录”

要添加此内容，请转到“视图”->“共享”下的“_Layout.cshtml”中的标题部分并进行以下更改。

但是等待单击“注册”和“登录”链接不起作用。 这是因为 Identity 是在 ASP.Net Core MVC 中实现的，带有“Razor”页面。 因此，我们有一个需要 Ragpr 页面支持的 MVC 应用程序，为此我们必须通过添加“app.MapRazorPages();”为我们的程序注入 razor 页面支持。 在 Program.cs 中

现在单击注册和登录按钮重定向到相应的页面。

步骤 03：默认情况下，在注册页面身份添加仅电子邮件、密码和确认密码字段。 但我们将添加“名字”和“姓氏”字段。 为此，我们首先需要“AspNetCoreIdentityUser”模型类中的“名字”和“姓氏”属性。

一旦我们设置了实体属性，我们就可以创建我们的数据库。 为此，请访问 appsettings.json 获取我们的连接字符串。 这里我们有一个由 scafollded 添加的默认连接。 这里我们有我们的服务器“mssqllocaldb”，它是一个本地数据库和数据库“AspNetCoreIdentity”。

步骤 04：让我们创建数据库。 首先，我们将添加一个迁移，然后我们将导入迁移并更新数据库。 这是一个 EF Core 概念。

添加迁移初始创建
更新数据库

添加迁移后，我们将看到添加了文件结构的以下“迁移”文件夹。

在“InitialCreate”文件中，我们可以看到我们的“First Name”和“Last Name”添加到“AspNetUsers”表中。


步骤 05：现在我们的数据库已准备就绪。 我们现在将在我们的注册页面和两个新字段“名字”和“姓氏”上工作。 首先，我们需要更改输入模型并添加“First Name”和“Last Name”的定义。 由于注册是一个剃须刀页面，我们将转到后端代码并进行以下更改。


现在转到 HTML 页面并添加这两个字段。


让我们构建应用程序转到注册页面，我们现在可以看到新字段。

步骤 06：在创建新用户之前，我们需要修改我们的后端，因为我们需要配置我们的名字和姓氏。 转到 Register.csghtml.cs 页面中的“OnPostAsync”并添加以下代码。

现在创建一个新用户：

我们的用户注册成功，这是临时确认页面。 点击确认账户。 稍后我们将禁用此电子邮件确认选项。

现在转到登录页面并输入我们的用户凭据并单击登录，我们成功登录到我们的用户。

步骤 07：现在我们将研究如何禁用电子邮件确认。 首先从您的应用程序中注销并转到 Program.cs。 在这里，我们将看到在 AddDefaultIdentity 部分中，我们将 RequireConfirmedAccount 设置为 false。

现在构建应用程序并转到注册页面并创建另一个新用户。

注册后，我们直接重定向到主页。

我们的数据在我们的数据库中如下所示：

步骤 08：现在让我们看看我们的身份特征。 单击用户名，这将带您进入管理个人资料页面。 在这里，您将看到管理我们的凭据的各种选项，例如个人资料、电子邮件、密码、双重身份验证、个人数据

修复匿名身份验证：

正如我们所看到的，用户可以成功登录，但我们的身份仍然是匿名的，正如我们从以下观察者窗口中看到的来自基类的用户身份值。

为此，我们需要创建一个 cookie，服务器将查看此 cookie 以进行身份​​验证。 转到 Login.cshtml.cs 页面并在“OnPosyAsync”中添加以下声明、身份和声明原则。 然后使用 HttpContext.SignInAsync() 它将声明原则序列化为字符串并加密该字符串并将其作为 cookie 保存到 httpcontext 对象中。

现在，当您尝试登录时，您将获得以下异常页面。

修复这个转到 Program.cs

现在再次运行应用程序并登录，您现在可以看到您不再以匿名身份登录

结论：

这里我们实现了用户验证过程。 然后我们注入 cookie 身份验证处理程序，这样用户就不会以匿名身份登录。 ASP.NET Core provides many tools and libraries to secure ASP.NET Core apps such as built-in identity providers and third-party identity services such as Facebook, Twitter, and LinkedIn. ASP.NET Core 提供了许多工具和库来保护 ASP.NET Core 应用程序，例如内置身份提供程序和第三方身份服务，例如 Facebook、Twitter 和 LinkedIn。



原文:

In this blog post we will look into how to implement ASP.NET Core Identity in a web app for adding authentication and authorization feature. Users can create an account and login with a username and password. This article is especially focusing on newcomers and anyone new wants to learn or thinking of using Identity in their .NET program. This is a series of blog post so please look into other for more information.

What is ASP.NET Core Identity?

When we build an application one of the key factors is that "how to handle the user management?", that means manages users, login, logout, passwords, profile data, roles, claims, tokens, email confirmation and more. So, to help ease these user management process we can use ASP.NET Core Identity a membership system.

Development Setup

For this example, we will be creating a ASP.NET Core MVC Web App using Visual Studio 2022 and .NET 6.0

Step 01: When creating an ASP.NET Core Web App (MVC) we will not select the Authentication type and set it as "None" as we will be looking from scratch how to set up the Identity feature in the application. For simplicity we will uncheck the "Configure for HTTPS" and "Enable Docker"

After creating we will have a basing MVC web application without any authentication feature.

And running the project will show the following webpage:

Step 02: Now we will add Identity feature by Scaffolding. So, right click on the project and go to "ADD" and select "New Scaffolded Item". In the following window select "Identity" and press "Add". In order to scaffolded to run a nuget package "Microsoft.VisualStudio.Web.CodeGeneration.Design" need to install. This will get automatically install when adding scaffolded item.

From the following select the Identity features that we would like to add in our web app. For this example, we will be selecting "Login", "Logout", "Register". We also need to specify the following:

  • Layout page: a layout page for the identity files so, we will be selecting our existing shared "_Layout.cshtml" page that got created by default with the project.
  • Data Context Class: By selecting + button it will generate the context class name and we will rename it to "AspNetCoreIdentityDbContext"
  • User class: By selecting + button we are setting the user model class name to "AspNetCoreIdentityUser"

Click on "Add" now our identity scaffolded. And we have the following structure changes in the project

Here we have "AspNetCoreIdentityDbContext" for our database. By which we will access our SQL Server. Then we have "AspNetCoreIdentityUser" which inherits from "IdentityUser" which implements the code for Login, Logout, Register and more. Now run the app and we need to add "Register" "Login"

To add this go to the header section in "_Layout.cshtml" under "Views" -> "Shared" and made the following changes.

But wait clicking "Register" and "Login" link does not work. This is because Identity is implemented in ASP.Net Core MVC is with "Razor" pages. So, we have an MVC application which needs Ragpr page supports and for this we have to inject our program with razor page support by adding "app.MapRazorPages();" in the Program.cs

Now clicking Register and Login button redirects to the respective pages.

Step 03: By default in Register page Identity adds only Email, Password and confirm passowrd fields. But we will add "First Name" and "Last Name" field. To do this we first need to a "First Name" and "Last Name" property in the "AspNetCoreIdentityUser" model class.

Once we have the entity properties set we can create our database. For this go to appsettings.json for our connection string. Here we have a default connection added by scafollded. Here we have our server "mssqllocaldb" which is a local db and database "AspNetCoreIdentity".

![connection string(https://dev-to-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com/uploads/articles/7exjayqgmnwrlbtzip6v.png)

Step 04: Let's create the database. First we will add a migration then we will import the migration and update the database. This is a EF Core concept.

Add-Migration InitialCreate
Update-Database

After adding the migration we will see the following "Migration" folder added with the file structure.

And in the "InitialCreate" file we can see the our "First Name" and "Last Name" added to the "AspNetUsers" table.


Step 05: Now our database is ready. We will now work on our Register page and two new field "First Name" and "Last Name". First we need to change our input model and add definition for "First Name" and "Last Name". As Register is a razor page we will go to the backend code and make the following changes.


Now goto the HTML page and add these two fields.


Let's build the application goto Register page and we now see the new fields.

Step 06: Before creating a new user we need to modify our backend as we need to configure our firstname and lastname. Goto "OnPostAsync" in Register.csghtml.cs page and add the following code.

Now create a new user:

Our user is successfully registered and this is temporary confirmation page. Click confirm account. Later we will disable this email confirmation option.

Now goto Login page and put our user credential and click Login and we successfully login to our user.

Step 07: Now we will look into how to disable Email confirmation. First logout from your application and goto Program.cs. Here we will see that in AddDefaultIdentity section we will set RequireConfirmedAccount to false.

Now build the application and goto Register page and create another new user.

After registering we directly redirect to main page.

And our data looks like below in our database:

Step 08: Now lets look into our identity features. Click on username which will take you to the manage profile page. Here you will see vairous option to manage our credentials like Profile, Email, Password, Two-factor authentication, personal data

Fix Anonymous Authentication:

As we can see user can login successfully but our identity is still anonymous as we can see from the following watcher windows for user identity value from base class.

For this we need to create a cookie and server will look into this cookie to authenticate. Goto Login.cshtml.cs page and add the following Claim, Identity and Claims principle in "OnPosyAsync". Then use HttpContext.SignInAsync() which will serialize the claims principles into string and encrypt that string and save that as a cookie into httpcontext object.

Now when you try to login you will get the following exception page.

To fix this goto to Program.cs

Now run the app again and login you now can see that you are not anymore logged in as anonymous

Concluiton:

Here we implemented user verification process. Then we injected the cookie authentication handlers so user do not logged in as anonymous. ASP.NET Core provides many tools and libraries to secure ASP.NET Core apps such as built-in identity providers and third-party identity services such as Facebook, Twitter, and LinkedIn.
item.author: sabahshariq

item.title: 巴什很强大！ 让我们构建一个简单的猜数字游戏。 | Bash is powerful! Lets build a simple number guesser game.
item.description:
数字猜测器是一个不太复杂的项目，您将学习如何使用 if、else if、else 语句、函数、用户输入和随机模块（当然这些编程概念将以 Bash 方式完成！） . 等等，什么是 Bash？ 这意味着要精通 linux，它有助于精通 Bash。 此外，大多数（如果不是全部）Linux 发行版都支持 bash，因此无需下载任何新内容。 只是在运行中运行它。

好吧，让我们构建我们的游戏吧！ 这是计划！

首先，我们需要一种方法来生成一个随机数（我们将使用内置的 random 函数）并将其分配给一个变量，这样我们就可以引用该变量并将其与用户输入进行比较。 接下来我们需要获取用户输入。 然后最后我们需要将用户输入与随机数进行比较。 如果用户猜对了，那么就结束游戏并让用户知道他们赢了！ 否则我们需要继续游戏直到用户获胜。

好简单，让我们开始写代码吧！

首先，我们附加 shebang（sharp 和 bang 的缩写），然后使用我们的 /bin/bash 路径。 我们这样做是为了确保我们的 linux 操作系统正确执行 bash 文件。 它应该看起来像这样。

接下来我们需要一种通过命令行读取用户输入的方法。 为此，请使用内置的读取命令。 好吧，将其附加到 -p 以将其分配给变量（这样我们以后可以引用它）。 这应该看起来像这样。

 read -p "What number would you like to pick?" user_guess

所以读取 -p（读取命令），“你想选择什么数字”（打印的字符串），然后是 user_guess（带有赋值的变量名）。

接下来我们需要将用户输入与随机数进行比较，以确定玩家是否正确。 为此，请使用 if/else 语句。 在 bash 中，这个逻辑语句看起来像这样。

 if [ conditional] then ( do something here ) else ( do something ) fi

我们还想把东西打印到终端上，让用户知道他是否正确。 对于这口井使用回声。 回声看起来像这样。

 echo "this string will be printed to the terminal screen"

所以结合 echo 和 if 语句，我们的实际代码应该是这样的。

 if [ ${ random_number } == ${ user_guess } ] then echo "You guessed right!" else echo "You guessed wrong" fi

惊人的！ 让我们构建最终产品！

现在我们已经整理好了基本的逻辑，从这里我将清理一下代码并使其更具可读性。 我还将向函数添加一些逻辑并调用该函数，因为（在我看来）它使所有内容都更具可读性，但更重要的是，如果当前用户猜错了，我将使用递归来调用该函数。 所以这里是成品！

 echo "Random number guesser!" read -p "What is the random number you would like to guess? Please pick a number between 0 and 9" user_guess function number_guess () { if [ ${ random_number } == ${ user_guess } ] then echo "You guessed right! Congratulations ${ random_number } is equal to ${ user_guess } " else echo "You guessed wrong! Try again." read -p "What is the random number you would like to guess? please pick a number between 0 and 9" user_guess number_guess fi } number_guess

如您所见，构建一个简单的猜数字游戏并不难。 它只需要几行代码，因为我们使用了 bash，所以这个脚本非常容易从命令行运行。

感谢您阅读我的文章！

想知道我在做什么？ 您可以在http://davidesquerra.com/或https://github.com/Davidfree2上查看我

如果您真的喜欢这篇文章，请在下方鼓掌，甚至关注！ 让我知道你的想法，如果你想看到更多这样的内容。 如果有不够清楚的地方，也请提出问题。 感谢您的阅读。



原文:

A number guesser is a less complex project to build and you’ll learn how to work with if, else if, else statements, functions, user input, and the random module (Of course these programming concepts will be done the Bash way!). Wait but what is Bash? Bash is the default login shell for most linux systems. Meaning that to get good at linux it helps to get good at Bash. Bash can be ran straight from the linux terminal and can also be added to a text file and made executable using the linux command line on the spot! Also most (if not all) linux distros support bash so no need to download anything new. Just run it on the fly.

Alright lets build our game! Here’s the plan!

First we need a way to generate a random number (we’ll use the built in random function) and assign it to a variable that way we can reference the variable and compare it to the user input. Next we need to get user input. Then finally we need to compare the user input to the random number. If the user is guessed right then well end the game and let the user know they won! Else we need to continue the game until the user wins.

Alright simple enough, Lets start writing the code!

First we append the shebang (which is short for sharp and bang) and follow that with our /bin/bash path. We do this to ensure that our linux operating system executes the bash file properly. It should look like this.

Next we need a way to read user input through the command line. For this well use the built in read command. Well append that with -p to assign it to a variable (that way we can reference it later). This should look like this.

read -p "What number would you like to pick?" user_guess

So read -p (read command), “what number would you like to pick” (the printed string), then user_guess (the variable name with assigned value).

Next we need to compare the user input to the random number to determine whether or not the game player is right. For this well use if/else statements. If/else statements check if a condition is true and will execute a code block depending on condition. In bash this logical statements looks like this.

if [conditional] then (do something here) else (do something) fi

We also want to print things to the terminal to let the user know whether or not hes correct. For this well use echo. Echo (actually echo lowercase) is another built in command and is used to print strings to the terminal. Echo looks like this.

echo "this string will be printed to the terminal screen"

So combining echo and our if statement, our actual code should look like this.

if [ ${random_number} == ${user_guess} ]
then echo "You guessed right!"
else echo "You guessed wrong"
fi

Awesome! Lets build the final product!

Now that we have the bare bones logic in order, from here i’ll clean up the code a little and make it more readable. I’ll also add some logic to a function and call that function because (in my opinion) it makes everything more readable, But more importantly I will be using recursion to recall the function if the current user guesses wrong. So here’s the finished product!

echo "Random number guesser!" read -p "What is the random number you would like to guess? Please pick a number between 0 and 9" user_guess function number_guess() { if [ ${random_number} == ${user_guess} ] then echo "You guessed right! Congratulations ${random_number} is equal to ${user_guess}" else echo "You guessed wrong! Try again." read -p "What is the random number you would like to guess? please pick a number between 0 and 9" user_guess number_guess fi }                                                                                                   

number_guess

As you can see building a simple number guessing game isn’t super difficult. It just takes a few lines of code and because we utilized bash this script is super easy to run from the command line.

Thank you for reading my article!

Wondering what i’m up to? You can check me out here at http://davidesquerra.com/ or here at https://github.com/Davidfree2

If you really enjoyed this article drop a clap down below or even a follow! Let me know what you think and if you want to see more content like this. Drop a question too if there’s something that’s not clear enough. Thank you for reading.
item.author: davidfree2

item.title: 不要担心响应式设计。 | Don't worry about responsive design.
item.description:
作为前端开发人员，响应式设计对于理解这一点至关重要。 有了这个，这是我的两分钱。

什么是响应式设计？

当我第一次开始开发网站时，“响应式设计”这个词不断出现。 我最初对响应式设计的想法是网站加载或响应用户的速度。 事实并非如此。 根据我收集到的信息，前端 Web 开发人员必须了解这一点。 这是因为网站需要以尽可能多的屏幕尺寸进行访问。 记住响应式设计意味着什么的最简单方法是，网站如何响应当前显示的屏幕尺寸。

首先学习基础知识

作为初学者，当您刚刚学习如何编写 HTML 和 CSS 时，担心响应式设计会变得不知所措和令人沮丧。

我的建议是先学习基础知识并为特定的屏幕尺寸创建一个网页。 专注于单一屏幕尺寸将使您的头脑围绕 HTML 和 CSS。 当您知道自己能够创建网页而不必担心它在所有屏幕尺寸上的响应速度时，您最终会感觉更有成就感。

根据需要创建尽可能多的网站

建立对 HTML 和 CSS 的基本理解，您可以轻松地重新创建简单的移动或桌面网站设计。 一旦您感到舒适，响应式设计的理念就会更容易掌握，因为您将了解网页上的文本、图像、链接和布局是如何被操纵的。 有了这种力量，根据屏幕尺寸更改这些元素对您来说会更加清晰。

结论

当您第一次学习 Web 开发时，不要担心如何创建响应式网站。 相反，使用 HTML 和 CSS 创建静态网站布局更为重要。 一旦您对此感到满意，那么在您的网站中加入响应能力的想法就会更加清晰。

一旦暂停，avieldez 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦未发表，avieldez 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 avieldez 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

原文:

Responsive design is essential to understand as a front-end developer. With that out of the way here's my two cents.

What is responsive design?

When I first started develop websites the term, "responsive design" kept popping up. My initial thought of responsive design was how quickly a website loads, or responds to the user. That was not the case. From what I gathered, it is essential for front-end web developers to understand. This is because websites need to be accessible with as many screen sizes as possible. The easiest way to remember what responsive design means is, how the website RESPONDS to the screen size it is currently being displayed on.

Learn the basics first

As a beginner, when you are just learning how to write HTML and CSS, worrying about responsive design can become overwhelming and frustrating.

My advice is to learn the basics and create a webpage for a specific screen size first. Focusing on a single screen size will allow to wrap your head around HTML and CSS. You'll end up feeling more accomplished in knowing you are capable of creating a webpage without having to worry about how responsive it is across all screen sizes.

Create as many websites as you need

Establish a base understanding of HTML and CSS where you are able to recreate a simple mobile or desktop website design with ease. Once you feel comfortable, the ideas of responsive design will be much easier to grasp as you will understand how text, images, links and layouts on the webpage are manipulated. With this power in your hands, changing those elements depending on screen sizes will be much clearer to you.

Conclusion

When you are first learning web development don't worry about how to create responsive websites. Instead, it is more important to create a static website layout using HTML and CSS. Once you feel comfortable with this then the ideas of incorporating responsiveness in your websites will much clearer.

item.title: 十二个时钟 | A dozenal clock
item.description:
世界各地的数字时钟每天使用 24 小时来显示时间。 在晚上，一天从 00:00、00:59、01:00、01:59、02:00 开始，一直到 23:00 和 23:59。 模拟时钟从 12 点（时钟的顶部）开始显示夜晚。 大指针从 12, 1, 2, ..., 11 一直移动到 12 后，一个小时过去了，小指针指向 1。这样一直重复到下午，此时同一时间显示为半夜，双手指向12。又过了12个小时，又是黑夜，白昼过去了。

一些国家使用 AM 和 PM 来表示时间。 一天的第一个小时是中午 12 点（晚上），然后是凌晨 1 点、凌晨 2 点，直到凌晨 12 点（早上结束），然后变成下午 1 点、下午 2 点等，直到晚上 12 点。 该符号遵循模拟时钟。

问题

很长一段时间以来，我一直想知道为什么时钟会这样工作。 我问自己以下问题：

  1. 为什么一天中的第一个小时命名为 12 PM，而接下来的几个小时从 1、2、3 等开始？
  2. 为什么下午不是从 10:00 开始（小时的第一个数字从 0 变为 1）？
  3. 为什么我们一小时有 60 分钟，而模拟时钟却分为 12 组，每组 5 分钟？
  4. 如果数字时间是 13:11，为什么我在模拟时钟上找不到这些数字？
  5. 为什么这些时间看起来不相似？
  6. 为什么模拟时钟的时钟上有三个数字，两个数字，而其他数字只有一个数字？

这些问题让我开始思考时间、小时和分钟的划分，以及数字和模拟时钟上小时和分钟的表示。

代替以 10 为基数（十进制系统）编写数字，我们将以 12 为基数（十进制系统）编写数字。

为此，我们需要两个新符号来表示十进制数字 10 和 11。

遵循使用 12 进制或十二进制数字（英国、美国）的全球社会，我们将使用 ↊ 和 ↋ 符号。 它们发音为dek和el 。 前十二个数字是：1、2、3、4、5、6、7、8、9、↊、↋ 和 10。

数字 ↊ 和 ↋ 在七段显示器（在许多数字时钟中使用）上很容易、唯一且可识别地表示。 它们可以表示为🯲和🯳，但转了180度。 甚至还有一个支持打数的dozenal包打数。 维基百科有一篇关于打系统及其历史的综合文章。

书写时间的新方法

我们将使用打数系统为数字和模拟时钟创建更好的表示。

对于数字时钟，使用以下系统：

  • 第一个数字表示晚上/早上为0 ，下午/晚上为1 。
  • 第二个数字表示小时，从0到↋ 。
  • 小时和分钟由一个·分隔，虽然:也被接受。
  • 第三位数字表示一小时内 5 分钟组的数量，从0到↋ 。
  • 第四个数字表示 5 分钟组中的分钟，从0到4 。

第二个和第三个数字可以通过字体大小或字体粗细（粗体）来强调，尽管这不是必需的。

然后一整天在数字时钟上看起来如下：

  • 第一分钟是00·00 。
  • 第二分钟是00·01 。
  • 第五分钟是00·04 。
  • 第六分钟是00·10 。
  • 第二个小时的第一分钟是01:00 。
  • 中午前的最后一分钟是0↋:↋4 。
  • 中午是10:00 。
  • 一天的最后一分钟是0↋:↋4 。
  • 闰秒将表示为20:00 。

对于模拟时钟，从 0 到 ↋ 的数字在时钟周围均匀分布。 数字 0 在顶部。

对于模拟时钟，我们有以下时间：

  • 请注意，这些示例中省略了秒，但可以附加它们并以与表示分钟相同的方式表示它们。

    考虑一下表示。 通常表示为 AM / PM 的内容现在可以看作是第一位数字。 早晚时间0↊·00和1↊·00表示为几乎相同的时间。 略读时间，通常只需要查看第二个和第三个数字，而不是所有四个数字。 一刻钟可以用数字0 、 3 、 6和9来识别。

    在实践中

    这个理论很好，但我决定在我的生活中尽可能多地把这个理论付诸实践。

    对于我的数字手表（Garmin 设备，带有可编程钟面），我制作了一个显示十进制时间的 Garmin 表盘。 在 Connect IQ 上查看应用程序。

    我办公室的墙上有一个自制的模拟时钟。 它是用一块钢板激光切割而成的。

    我的笔记本电脑运行 Ubuntu，它的系统时钟在我的任务栏中使用十几个时钟可视化。

    还有其他地方我可能会实现十几个时钟来替换十进制时钟，例如：

    如果您有更多想法，请告诉我！



原文:

Digital clocks around the world use 24 hours each day to display the time. In the night the day starts as 00:00, 00:59, 01:00, 01:59, 02:00, up to 23:00 and 23:59. (Actually, digital time goes up to 24:00 when the day has a leap second.)

Analog clocks display the night starting at 12 (top of the clock). After the large hand has traveled all the way from 12, 1, 2, ..., 11 and back to 12, an hour has passed and the small hand points at 1. This repeats until afternoon, when the same time displays as the middle of the night and both hands point to the 12. After another 12 hours it is night again and the day has passed.

Some countries use AM and PM to denote the time. There the first hour of the day is 12PM (in the night), then 1AM, 2AM, until 12AM (end of the morning), which turns to 1PM, 2PM etc. until 12PM in the night. This notation follows the analog clock.

Questions

For a long time I have wondered why clocks work this way. I asked myself the following questions:

  1. Why is the fist hour of the day named 12 PM, and the following hours start at 1, 2, 3, etc.?
  2. Why does the afternoon not start at 10:00 (when the first digit in the hours changes from 0 to 1)?
  3. Why do we have 60 minutes in an hour, but are analog clocks divided into 12 sets of 5 minutes?
  4. If the digital time is 13:11, why can't I find these numbers on an analog clock?
  5. Both 10:00 and 22:00 point at the same number on an analog clock. Why don't these times look similar?
  6. Why does an analog clock have three numbers on the clock which have two digits, while the other numbers have one digit?

These questions got me thinking about time, the division of hours and minutes, and the representation of hours and minutes on a digital and analog clock.

Introduction to base-12

Instead of writing numbers in base 10 (decimal system), we will write numbers in base 12, the dozenal system.

To do that we need two new symbols to represent the decimal numbers 10 and 11.

Following the worldwide society that uses base-12 or duodecimal numbers (British, American), we shall use the ↊ and ↋ symbols. They are pronounced dek and el. (You may also find the Greek symbols χ and ξ used for dek and el, but the ↊ and ↋ symbols have been officially accepted into Unicode since 2015.)

The first twelve numbers are then: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, ↊, ↋ and 10.

The digits ↊ and ↋ are easly and uniquely and recognizably representable on a seven-segment display (used in many digital clocks). They can be respresented as 🯲 and 🯳, but turned around 180 degrees. The 🯲 is the same upside down, and the top bar can be removed to make it unique.

There is even a LaTeX package dozenal that supports dozenal numbers. Wikipedia has a comprehensive article about the dozenal system and its history.

A new way to write the time

We will use the dozenal number system to create a better representation for clocks, both digial and analog.

For digital clocks, the following system is used:

  • The first digit denotes the night/morning as 0 and the afternoon/evening as 1.
  • The second digit denotes the hour, 0 up to ↋.
  • The hours and minutes are separated by a ·, although : is also accepted.
  • The third digit denotes the number of 5-minute groups in the hour, 0 up to ↋.
  • The fourth digit denotes the minute in the 5-minute group, 0 up to 4.

The second and third digits can be emphasized either by font size, or by font weight (bold font), although it is not required.

A whole day then looks as the following on a digital clock:

  • The first minute is 00·00.
  • The second minute is 00·01.
  • The fifth minute is 00·04.
  • The sixth minute is 00·10.
  • ... and so on until the last minute of the first hour is 00:↋4.
  • The first minute of the of the second hour is 01:00.
  • The last minute before noon is 0↋:↋4.
  • Noon is 10:00.
  • The last minute of the day is 0↋:↋4.
  • A leap second would be represented as 20:00.

For analog clocks, the numbers from 0 to ↋ are spaced evenly around the clock. The number 0 is at the top.

For analog clocks we have the following times:

  • 🕛 is 00·00
  • 🕧 is 00·60
  • 🕐 is 01·00
  • 🕜 is 01·60
  • 🕤 is 09·60
  • 🕙 is 0↊·00
  • 🕦 is 0↋·60

Note that seconds are left out of these examples, but they can be appended and represented the same way as the minutes are represented.

Think about the representation for a bit. What is often denoted as AM / PM can now be seen as the first digit. The morning and evening times 0↊·00 and 1↊·00 are represented as almost the same time. Skimming the time, it is usually only neccesary to look at the second and third digit instead of all four digits. The quarters of the hour can be identified by the digits 0, 3, 6 and 9.

In practice

The theory is nice, but I decided to put the theory into practice as well in my life, in as many places as possible.

For my digital watch (a Garmin device, with programmable clock faces) I made a Garmin watch face that displays the dozenal time. View the app on Connect IQ.

The wall in my office has a self-made dozenal analog clock. It has been laser-cut from a sheet of steel.

My laptop runs Ubuntu and its system clock is visualized in my task bar using a dozenal clock.

There are other places where I might implement dozenal clocks to replace the decimal clocks, for example:

Let me know if you have more ideas!
item.author: hiddewie

item.title: Go-1 中的并发性 | Concurrency in Go-1
item.description:
尼拉吉·库马尔

发表于什么是并发？
并发是一次处理很多事情的能力。 最好用一个例子来解释。

Let's consider a person jogging. During his morning jog, let's say his shoelaces become untied. Now the person stops running, ties his shoelaces and then starts running again. This is a classic example of concurrency. The person is capable of handling both running and tying shoelaces, that is the person is able to deal with lots of things at once :)

并发性和并行性 - 技术观点

大程序分为较小的子程序。 同时运行其较小组件的程序称为并发。

协程


A goroutine is a lightweight execution thread in the Go programming language and a function that executes concurrently with the rest of the program.

Goroutines are incredibly cheap when compared to traditional threads as the overhead of creating a goroutine is very low. Therefore, they are widely used in Go for concurrent programming.

To invoke a function as a goroutine, use the go keyword.
句法
去 foo()
We write go before the function foo to invoke it as a goroutine. The function foo() will run concurrently or asynchronously with the calling function.

例子：

 package main import ( "fmt" "time" ) // Prints numbers from 1-3 along with the passed string func foo(s string) { for i := 1; i <= 3; i++ { time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond) fmt.Println(s, ": ", i) } } func main() { // Starting two goroutines go foo("1st goroutine") go foo("2nd goroutine") // Wait for goroutines to finish before main goroutine ends time.Sleep(time.Second) fmt.Println("Main goroutine finished") }

例子：

 package main import ( "fmt" "strconv" ) func main() { ch := make(chan string) for i := 0; i < 10; i++ { go func(i int) { for j := 0; j < 10; j++ { ch <- "Goroutine : " + strconv.Itoa(i) } }(i) } for k := 1; k <= 100; k++ { fmt.Println(k, <-ch) } }

从 Channel 发送和接收值主要函数有两个函数生成器和接收器。 我们创建 ac int 通道并从生成器函数中返回它。 在匿名 goroutine 中运行的 for 循环将值写入通道 c。 

 package main import ( "fmt" ) func main() { c := generator() receiver(c) } func receiver(c <-chan int) { for v := range c { fmt.Println(v) } } func generator() <-chan int { c := make(chan int) go func() { for i := 0; i < 10; i++ { c <- i } close(c) }() return c }

启动多个 Goroutine

 package main import ( "fmt" "time" ) func numbers() { for i := 1; i <= 5; i++ { time.Sleep(250 * time.Millisecond) fmt.Printf("%d ", i) } } func alphabets() { for i := 'a'; i <= 'e'; i++ { time.Sleep(400 * time.Millisecond) fmt.Printf("%c ", i) } } func main() { go numbers() go alphabets() time.Sleep(3000 * time.Millisecond) fmt.Println("main terminated") }

这就是 Goroutines 的内容。 祝你有美好的一天。

一旦暂停，neeraj1997dev 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，neeraj1997dev 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，neeraj1997dev 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 neeraj1997dev 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 neeraj1997dev 的方法：

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 neeraj1997dev 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Neeraj Kumar

Posted on Oct 8

Go is a concurrent language and not a parallel one. Before discussing how concurrency is taken care in Go, we must first understand what is concurrency and how it is different from parallelism.
What is concurrency?
Concurrency is the capability to deal with lots of things at once. It's best explained with an example.

Let's consider a person jogging. During his morning jog, let's say his shoelaces become untied. Now the person stops running, ties his shoelaces and then starts running again. This is a classic example of concurrency. The person is capable of handling both running and tying shoelaces, that is the person is able to deal with lots of things at once :)

Concurrency and Parallelism - A technical point of view

Large programs are divided into smaller sub-programs. Programs which run their smaller components at the same time is known as concurrency.

Goroutines


A goroutine is a lightweight execution thread in the Go programming language and a function that executes concurrently with the rest of the program.

Goroutines are incredibly cheap when compared to traditional threads as the overhead of creating a goroutine is very low. Therefore, they are widely used in Go for concurrent programming.

To invoke a function as a goroutine, use the go keyword.
Syntax
go foo()
We write go before the function foo to invoke it as a goroutine. The function foo() will run concurrently or asynchronously with the calling function.

example:

package main
import (
    "fmt"
    "time"
)

// Prints numbers from 1-3 along with the passed string
func foo(s string) {
    for i := 1; i <= 3; i++ {
        time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
        fmt.Println(s, ": ", i)
    }
}

func main() {

    // Starting two goroutines
    go foo("1st goroutine")
    go foo("2nd goroutine")

    // Wait for goroutines to finish before main goroutine ends
    time.Sleep(time.Second)
    fmt.Println("Main goroutine finished")
}

example:

package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "strconv"
)

func main() {
    ch := make(chan string)

    for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
        go func(i int) {
            for j := 0; j < 10; j++ {
                ch <- "Goroutine : " + strconv.Itoa(i)
            }
        }(i)
    }

    for k := 1; k <= 100; k++ {
        fmt.Println(k, <-ch)
    }
}

https://replit.com/@neerajkumar1997/Goroutines?v=1

Send and Receive values from Channel The main function has two functions generator and receiver. We create a c int channel and return it from the generator function. The for loop running inside anonymous goroutines writing the values to the channel c.

package main

import (
    "fmt"
)

func main() {
    c := generator()
    receiver(c)
}

func receiver(c <-chan int) {
    for v := range c {
        fmt.Println(v)
    }
}

func generator() <-chan int {
    c := make(chan int)

    go func() {
        for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
            c <- i
        }
        close(c)
    }()

    return c
}

https://replit.com/@neerajkumar1997/Send-and-Receive-values-from-Channel?v=1

Starting multiple Goroutine

package main

import (  
    "fmt"
    "time"
)

func numbers() {  
    for i := 1; i <= 5; i++ {
        time.Sleep(250 * time.Millisecond)
        fmt.Printf("%d ", i)
    }
}
func alphabets() {  
    for i := 'a'; i <= 'e'; i++ {
        time.Sleep(400 * time.Millisecond)
        fmt.Printf("%c ", i)
    }
}
func main() {  
    go numbers()
    go alphabets()
    time.Sleep(3000 * time.Millisecond)
    fmt.Println("main terminated")
}

https://replit.com/@neerajkumar1997/Starting-multiple-Goroutines?v=1

That's it for Goroutines. Have a good day.
